commands.install:
go install github.com/8op-org/gl1tch@latest

Binary lands in $GOPATH/bin. Make sure it's on your $PATH.

Then pull a local model:

  ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:latest

Run `glitch`. That's it.
commands.pipelines:
Pipelines are YAML files that chain steps. Each step runs a model or tool. Each step gets the previous step's output.

name: code-review
version: "1"
steps:
  - id: read
    executor: ollama
    model: qwen2.5-coder:latest
    prompt: |
      Read the diff at {{input}} and summarize
      what changed. Be specific.

  - id: review
    executor: claude
    model: claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
    prompt: |
      Based on this summary:
      {{steps.read.output}}

      Flag any bugs, security issues, or
      patterns that look wrong.

Local model reads. Cloud model judges. Run it:

  glitch pipeline run code-review.yaml
commands.brain:
Your assistant remembers. Steps with write_brain: true store what they learn, scoped to your project. Future steps get that context automatically — no re-explaining, no re-indexing.

Example:

  - id: index
    executor: ollama
    model: qwen2.5-coder:latest
    write_brain: true
    prompt: Summarize the architecture of this codebase.

  - id: advise
    executor: claude
    model: claude-sonnet-4-6
    use_brain: true
    prompt: Given the architecture above, what should we tackle first?

Browse your brain notes: ^space b
commands.model:
Switch models from inside GLITCH:

  /model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:latest
  /model claude/claude-sonnet-4-6
  /model claude/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

Or open the picker: /models

Local models (Ollama) run on your machine — zero cost, nothing leaves.
Cloud models are one flag per step. Use them when the question earns it.
commands.ollama:
Ollama runs AI models locally. No API key. No cost. Prompts never leave your machine.

  brew install ollama
  ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:latest
  ollama pull llama3.2
  ollama serve

In any pipeline step:

  executor: ollama
  model: qwen2.5-coder:latest

From inside GLITCH: /model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:latest

qwen2.5-coder is the default for code tasks. llama3.2 is good for reasoning.
commands.why:
Every SaaS AI tool runs your code on someone else's server.

GLITCH defaults to local. Your prompts go nowhere. No API bill for the work that doesn't need one.

Cloud models are opt-in, one field per step. When a task genuinely needs Claude's reasoning — a final review, a hard synthesis — you add one line. The rest stays local.

You own the stack. The cost reflects the actual work.
commands.themes:
Themes are bundles: a palette YAML, an optional font, an optional splash screen.

Drop the directory in ~/.config/glitch/themes/ and switch from inside GLITCH:

  /themes

Arrow keys to browse, Enter to apply.
commands.cost:
Local models via Ollama cost nothing. No per-token charges. No seat license. No rate limits.

Cloud models are opt-in — one executor field in one step. Use them where they actually earn it.

Most pipelines: Ollama for the indexing and parsing, Claude for the final synthesis. The bill is tiny.
commands.cron:
Pipelines can run on a schedule. Daily standup prep. Nightly codebase summaries. Morning PR digests.

From inside GLITCH: /cron

Cron jobs keep running in your session. Results are waiting when you get back.
commands.docs:
Full documentation, right here:

  /docs

Browse every topic — getting started, pipelines, brain, plugins, themes, and more.
Use arrow keys or click to navigate. Esc to close.
commands.help:
Commands:

  /install     get GLITCH running
  /docs        browse the full documentation
  /pipelines   chain models with memory
  /brain       persistent context across runs
  /model       switch models
  /ollama      local models, zero cost
  /why         the philosophy
  /themes      visual customization
  /cost        what you actually pay
  /cron        scheduled pipelines

Or type a question. Tab completes commands.

qa.question: what is glitch
qa.response: GLITCH is your personal AI assistant — it lives in your terminal, learns your projects, and runs your automations. Local models run by default: your code stays on your machine, costs stay near zero. Cloud models are opt-in, per step, when they earn it. Talk to it like a chat interface. Run pipelines. Schedule automations. Make it yours.

qa.question: how to install glitch
qa.response:
go install github.com/8op-org/gl1tch@latest

Make sure $GOPATH/bin is on your $PATH. Then pull a local model:

  ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:latest

Run `glitch`. Type /install for the full walkthrough.

qa.question: how do I talk to glitch
qa.response: Run `glitch`. The input is at the bottom of the screen. Type your question and hit Enter. Type / to see slash commands — autocomplete shows all of them.

qa.question: what slash commands are there
qa.response:
Type / in GLITCH to see them all with autocomplete. The main ones:

  /pipeline [name]   launch a pipeline
  /model [name]      switch provider/model
  /models            open the model picker
  /themes            open the theme picker
  /prompt [name]     load a saved prompt
  /terminal [cmd]    open a terminal split
  /rerun [name]      rerun a pipeline
  /cron              open the scheduler
  /cwd [path]        change working directory
  /clear             clear chat history
  /quit              exit

qa.question: what are the keyboard shortcuts
qa.response:
Inside GLITCH:

  Tab         focus the input
  Esc         unfocus
  ^space a    focus the assistant (chord)
  ^space p    open pipeline builder
  ^space b    open brain editor
  ^space j    jump to a session window

Type / for slash command autocomplete.

qa.question: how do pipelines work
qa.response: Pipelines are YAML files that chain steps — each step gets the previous step's output. Local model for the indexing, cloud model for the synthesis. Run with `/pipeline name` from inside GLITCH, or `glitch pipeline run file.yaml` from the shell. Type /pipelines for a working example.

qa.question: what is brain context
qa.response: Brain is your assistant's memory. Steps with write_brain: true store what they learn, scoped to your project. Steps with use_brain: true get that context injected automatically — no re-explaining, no re-indexing. One run to learn, free context forever after. Type /brain for the full walkthrough.

qa.question: how do I switch models
qa.response:
/model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:latest
/model claude/claude-sonnet-4-6
/model claude/claude-haiku-4-5-20251001

Or open the full picker: /models

qa.question: how do I use local AI models
qa.response:
Install Ollama, pull a model, run `ollama serve`. Then from GLITCH: /model ollama/qwen2.5-coder:latest

Or set it in any pipeline step: executor: ollama, model: qwen2.5-coder:latest

Zero cost. Works offline. Your prompts never leave the machine. Type /ollama for the setup.

qa.question: how much does glitch cost
qa.response: GLITCH is free. Local models via Ollama cost nothing. You pay only for cloud API calls you explicitly add. Most workflows use Ollama for the heavy lifting and Claude for the last step. The bill is small. Type /cost for the full picture.

qa.question: how do themes work
qa.response: Type /themes from inside GLITCH. Arrow keys to browse, Enter to apply. Themes live in ~/.config/glitch/themes/ — each contains a palette YAML, an optional font, and an optional splash screen. Drop your own in and it shows up instantly.

qa.question: how do I create a pipeline
qa.response: Open the pipeline builder: ^space p from inside GLITCH. Or write a YAML file directly — name, version, and a steps list. Each step needs an id, executor, and prompt. Run it with /pipeline name or `glitch pipeline run file.yaml`. Type /pipelines for an annotated example.

qa.question: can pipelines run on a schedule
qa.response: Yes. From inside GLITCH: /cron to open the scheduler. Configure any pipeline to run daily, nightly, or on any cron schedule. Standup prep, nightly summaries, morning PR digests — results are waiting when you get back. Type /cron for the setup.

qa.question: what providers does glitch support
qa.response: Ollama for local models — qwen2.5-coder, llama3.2, mistral, codestral, anything you can pull. Claude via the Anthropic API. Mix them in a single pipeline. Local handles the cheap work, cloud handles the hard parts.

qa.question: does glitch work on linux
qa.response: macOS and Linux. Requires tmux in your environment. Windows via WSL2.

qa.question: is glitch open source
qa.response: GLITCH is source-available. See github.com/8op-org/gl1tch for the license and contribution guide.

qa.question: how do I configure glitch
qa.response: GLITCH reads ~/.config/glitch/config.yaml on startup. Set your default model, theme, and keybindings. Pipelines live in ~/.config/glitch/pipelines/.

qa.question: how do I get help
qa.response: Type /help to see all commands. Type /docs to open the full documentation. Type a question in plain text for fuzzy search. Open an issue at github.com/8op-org/gl1tch for bugs.

qa.question: how do I read the docs
qa.response: Type /docs to open the full documentation inline. Browse every topic — getting started, pipelines, brain, plugins, themes, scheduling, and more. Or visit the /docs page on the site.
